data.title: Is Alarm.com Holdings (ALRM) Pricing Reflect Its Recent Multi‑Year Share Price Declines
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Alarm.com Holdings is attractively priced at its current US$45.20 share price, the valuation story is more nuanced than a quick glance at the chart might suggest.
  • The stock has seen a 0.9% decline over the past week, an 8.3% decline over the past month, and is down 11.8% year to date. This extends to a 25.4% decline over 1 year and 49.1% over 5 years, which can change how investors think about both potential upside and risk.
  • These moves are occurring as the company continues to attract ongoing investor interest as a listed security, and as coverage like this aims to provide context for its current pricing. With no single headline event driving the trend, it is especially important to understand whether the share price still lines up with fundamentals.
  • Our valuation model gives Alarm.com Holdings a score of 5 out of 6, suggesting it screens as undervalued on most of the checks we run. Next, we will walk through the different valuation approaches behind that result before finishing with a more holistic way to think about the company’s value.

Find out why Alarm.com Holdings's -25.4% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Alarm.com Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to the present. It is essentially asking what those future dollars are worth in today’s terms.

For Alarm.com Holdings, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections rather than earnings multiples. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about $157.8 million. Looking ahead, analyst inputs and extrapolated estimates suggest free cash flow of $203.1 million in 2026 and $221.35 million in 2027, with further projections extending out to 2035, all in dollar terms and remaining below $1 billion per year.

When these projected cash flows are discounted back and combined with a terminal value, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $73.45 per share. Compared with the current share price of $45.20, this implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 38.5%, which indicates the stock screens as undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Alarm.com Holdings is undervalued by 38.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Alarm.com Holdings.

Approach 2: Alarm.com Holdings Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It links the share price directly to current earnings, which many investors use as a quick yardstick for how the market is pricing a business.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk tends to justify a lower multiple.

Alarm.com Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 17.59x. That sits below the Software industry average of 25.76x and well below the peer group average of 92.94x. Simply Wall St also uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio”, which is the P/E level suggested by factors such as the company’s earnings growth, profit margins, size, risk profile and its industry. For Alarm.com Holdings, this Fair Ratio is 21.24x, which aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry because it adjusts for company specific characteristics.

Compared with the Fair Ratio of 21.24x, the current P/E of 17.59x indicates that the shares screen as undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Alarm.com Holdings Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St's Community page you can use Narratives, where you spell out your story for Alarm.com Holdings in plain language, tie that story to concrete forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, and see the fair value it implies. You can then compare it to the current price to decide whether you think the stock looks expensive or cheap, with the tool automatically updating your view as new news or earnings arrive. data.title: BICO Group (OM:BICO) FY 2025 Loss Of SEK 1.6b Reinforces Profitability Concerns
data.description:
BICO Group (OM:BICO) has just posted its FY 2025 numbers, with fourth quarter revenue of SEK 451.3 million and a basic EPS loss of SEK 0.84, capping a trailing twelve month period where revenue reached SEK 1,497.2 million and basic EPS landed at a loss of SEK 22.38. The company has seen revenue move from SEK 1,946.3 million in the trailing twelve months to Q4 2024 to SEK 1,497.2 million in the latest trailing period, while EPS has shifted from a loss of SEK 1.28 to a loss of SEK 22.38. This sets up a results season where investors are likely to focus squarely on how much of that top line is flowing through to the bottom line. With shares trading at SEK 17.31, the story now centers on how much pressure remains on margins and whether the business can tighten its cost base over time.

See our full analysis for BICO Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the widely held narratives around BICO’s growth ambitions, loss profile, and what ultimately needs to change for margins to move in a more sustainable direction.

See what the community is saying about BICO Group

Advertisement

Losses remain heavy at SEK 1.6b over the year

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, BICO reported net income excluding extra items of a SEK 1.6b loss on SEK 1,497.2 million of revenue, with basic EPS at a loss of SEK 22.38, compared with a SEK 90.1 million loss and basic EPS loss of SEK 1.28 in the earlier trailing period to Q4 2024.
  • Bears argue that the widening loss profile is exactly what they are worried about, and these numbers give them plenty to point to:
    • Over the last five years, losses have grown at about 47.5% per year, and the latest trailing loss of about SEK 1.6b lines up with that picture of pressure on profitability rather than stabilization.
    • Analysts referenced in the cautious view are not expecting BICO to reach profitability in the next three years, so this latest SEK 1.6b loss can be read as consistent with their concern that the business may struggle to turn revenue into positive earnings in the near term.
If you want to see how skeptics frame these risks against future expectations and pricing, take a look at the detailed bear case for BICO in the 🐻 BICO Group Bear Case.

Quarterly EPS swings show how volatile results can be

  • Within FY 2025, basic EPS moved from a loss of SEK 3.32 in Q1 and a loss of SEK 2.59 in Q2 to a much larger loss of SEK 15.69 in Q3, before narrowing to a loss of SEK 0.84 in Q4, while net income excluding extra items ranged from a SEK 234.3 million loss in Q1 to a SEK 1,107.5 million loss in Q3 and a SEK 59.2 million loss in Q4.
  • Consensus narrative writers point out that BICO is working with large lab automation projects and a mix of software, hardware and services, and the pattern in these numbers lines up with that:
    • Q3 FY 2025 included earnings from discontinued operations of SEK 494.8 million alongside a SEK 1,107.5 million loss from ongoing operations, which supports the idea that one off moves and project timing can make individual quarters look very different from the broader FY 2025 picture.
    • The earlier FY 2024 quarters showed a similar mix, with Q4 2024 delivering SEK 570.8 million of revenue and SEK 258.4 million of net income excluding extra items while Q3 2024 had SEK 369.1 million of revenue and a SEK 260.8 million loss, again hinting at how the business can swing between profitable and loss making quarters as large projects and impairments land.

Low 0.8x P/S and DCF fair value of SEK 80.05 stand out

  • BICO trades on a P/S of about 0.8x, compared with an indicated 4.1x for peers and 3x for the broader European Life Sciences industry, while the supplied DCF fair value of SEK 80.05 sits well above the current share price of SEK 17.31.
  • Bulls highlight this valuation gap as a key part of their argument, but the earning profile means investors need to weigh it carefully:
    • On the supportive side, revenue is referenced as growing at around 6.7% per year in the analysis. When this is set against a 0.8x P/S, it is used by bullish investors to argue that BICO is priced more cheaply than many life sciences names with similar or lower growth expectations.
    • On the other hand, the same bullish narrative accepts that BICO is not forecast to become profitable in the next three years, so the SEK 80.05 DCF fair value and the analyst target of SEK 30.67 both assume that, over time, margins improve materially from the current loss making base to levels closer to the 11.1% industry margin.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Jackson Financial (JXN) After A 176.8% Three Year Run?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Jackson Financial's current share price really reflects its value, you are not alone and this article is here to unpack that question clearly.
  • The stock most recently closed at US$113.74, with returns of 1.5% over the last 7 days, 3.0% over 30 days, 5.8% year to date, 38.0% over 1 year and 176.8% over 3 years. This naturally raises questions about how much of this performance is already priced in.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Jackson Financial as investors reassess life insurance and retirement focused financial stocks and what they might offer in terms of income and capital appreciation. That context helps explain why the market is paying close attention to valuation signals rather than just headline share price moves.
  • On our checks, Jackson Financial currently scores a 4 out of 6 valuation score. This suggests it screens as undervalued on most but not all measures. Next we will look at how different valuation approaches view the stock, followed by an even more practical way to think about valuation at the end of the article.

Jackson Financial delivered 38.0% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Diversified Financial industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Jackson Financial Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value Jackson Financial creates over and above the return that equity investors require, rather than focusing solely on near term earnings or cash flows. It compares the company’s profitability on its equity base with the cost of that equity.

For Jackson Financial, the model uses a Book Value of $141.89 per share and a Stable EPS of $11.94 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The Average Return on Equity is 9.49%, while the Cost of Equity is $9.25 per share. That gap translates into an estimated Excess Return of $2.69 per share, using a Stable Book Value of $125.76 per share as a reference point from the past 5 years.

When these excess returns are projected and discounted, the Excess Returns model arrives at an intrinsic value of about $193.94 per share. Compared with the latest share price of US$113.74, this implies the stock trades at a 41.4% discount, which indicates that Jackson Financial screens as materially undervalued on this approach.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Jackson Financial is undervalued by 41.4%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 53 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Jackson Financial.

Approach 2: Jackson Financial Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Jackson Financial, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to link what you pay per share to the earnings that support that share price. It helps you see how much the market is willing to pay for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how investors see the company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower, more cautious multiple.

Jackson Financial currently trades on a P/E of 15.30x. That sits below the Diversified Financial industry average of 15.96x and also below the peer group average of 18.67x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Jackson Financial is 17.26x, which is the P/E level it would expect given factors such as earnings profile, industry, profit margin, market cap and identified risks.

This Fair Ratio can be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, because it accounts for company specific characteristics rather than treating all insurers and financials as the same. On this basis, Jackson Financial’s actual P/E of 15.30x is below the Fair Ratio of 17.26x. This suggests the shares may be undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 22 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Jackson Financial Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. On Simply Wall St’s Community page you can use Narratives, where you and other investors connect Jackson Financial’s story to a set of forecasts and a fair value, then compare that fair value to the current price. You can see it update automatically when fresh news or earnings arrive, and even sit at very different points on the spectrum. For example, one Narrative might anchor on a cautious US$68 price target with shrinking margins and higher perceived tail risk. Another might lean on a more optimistic US$137 fair value tied to capital strength, TPG’s partnership and a higher future earnings power, giving you a clear, side by side way to decide which story you think is more realistic.

For Jackson Financial however we'll make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Jackson Financial Narratives:

🐂 Jackson Financial Bull Case

Fair value used in this bullish narrative: US$137.00 per share

Implied discount to that fair value at the last close of US$113.74: about 17.0%

Revenue growth assumption used in the narrative model: 19.14%

  • Analysts in this camp see long term retirement trends and the shift toward annuity solutions as a supportive backdrop for Jackson’s diversified product set.
  • They highlight capital strength, risk controls and potential tail risk reduction as reasons the company could support higher earnings power over time.
  • Their valuation work links these assumptions to a higher fair value range, with updated targets up to US$137 and an expectation that capital and cash flow can support that view.

🐻 Jackson Financial Bear Case

Fair value used in this cautious narrative: US$100.00 per share

Implied premium to that fair value at the last close of US$113.74: about 13.7%

Revenue growth assumption used in the narrative model: 19.27%

  • Analysts here focus on tail risk from variable annuity guarantees, higher lapse rates and net outflows that could pressure cash flow and earnings stability.
  • They pay close attention to regulatory scrutiny around the captive reinsurer, reserve assumptions and potential balance sheet volatility.
  • Their fair value anchor of US$100 reflects a view that, even with growth and solid liquidity, earnings quality and guarantee risk could limit how high the valuation should sit.

Approach 1: Ormat Technologies Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required return rate.

For Ormat Technologies, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flows in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is a loss of $235 million, so the model assumes conditions change over time and uses analyst estimates as a starting point.

Analysts provide explicit forecasts up to 2027, with free cash flow projected at $35.95 million in 2026 and $54.2 million in 2027. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates further, with the ten year projection reaching about $139.06 million in 2035 based on the supplied growth assumptions.

After discounting these projected cash flows, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $32.85 per share. Versus a share price around $120, this suggests the stock is assessed as very expensive by this DCF, with an intrinsic discount figure indicating it is viewed as significantly overvalued within this framework.

Approach 2: Ormat Technologies Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to gauge how much investors are paying for each dollar of earnings. It ties the share price directly to the bottom line, which is usually what ultimately matters to equity holders.

A “normal” or “fair” P/E often reflects what the market is willing to pay for a company’s growth prospects and risk profile. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple.

Ormat Technologies currently trades on a P/E of about 54.7x. That is higher than the Renewable Energy industry average of roughly 16.2x and above the peer group average of about 37.2x. data.title: GMO TECH Holdings (TSE:415A) Is Posting Solid Earnings, But It Is Not All Good News
data.description:
Following the release of a positive earnings report recently, GMO TECH Holdings, Inc.'s (TSE:415A) stock performed well. Despite this, we feel that there are some reasons to be cautious with these earnings.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Examining Cashflow Against GMO TECH Holdings' Earnings

One key financial ratio used to measure how well a company converts its profit to free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio. To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

Over the twelve months to December 2025, GMO TECH Holdings recorded an accrual ratio of 3.00. That means it didn't generate anywhere near enough free cash flow to match its profit. Statistically speaking, that's a real negative for future earnings. In fact, it had free cash flow of JP¥37m in the last year, which was a lot less than its statutory profit of JP¥1.54b. Unfortunately, we don't have data on GMO TECH Holdings' free cash flow for the prior year; that's not necessarily a bad thing, though we do generally prefer to be able to see a bit of a company's history. Having said that, there is more to the story. We can see that unusual items have impacted its statutory profit, and therefore the accrual ratio.

See our latest analysis for GMO TECH Holdings

Note: we always recommend investors check balance sheet strength. Click here to be taken to our balance sheet analysis of GMO TECH Holdings.

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

The fact that the company had unusual items boosting profit by JP¥1.1b, in the last year, probably goes some way to explain why its accrual ratio was so weak. While we like to see profit increases, we tend to be a little more cautious when unusual items have made a big contribution. When we crunched the numbers on thousands of publicly listed companies, we found that a boost from unusual items in a given year is often not repeated the next year. And that's as you'd expect, given these boosts are described as 'unusual'. We can see that GMO TECH Holdings' positive unusual items were quite significant relative to its profit in the year to December 2025. All else being equal, this would likely have the effect of making the statutory profit a poor guide to underlying earnings power.

Our Take On GMO TECH Holdings' Profit Performance

GMO TECH Holdings had a weak accrual ratio, but its profit did receive a boost from unusual items. On reflection, the above-mentioned factors give us the strong impression that GMO TECH Holdings'underlying earnings power is not as good as it might seem, based on the statutory profit numbers.
